Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For most recent month-end performance information, visit www.impaxam.com

Figures include reinvested dividends, capital gains distribution, and changes in principal value.

1The inception date for Institutional Class is 04/02/2007 and Investor Class is 08/10/1971. As of 05/01/2024 prospectus, total annual operating expenses, gross of any fee waivers or reimbursements (excluding Acquired Fund fees and expenses), for Institutional Class and Investors Class shares are 0.67%, 0.92%, respectively.

The inception date for the Impax Sustainable Allocation Fund Institutional Class is April 2, 2007, and the Investor Class inception date is August 10, 1971. The performance information shown for Institutional Class shares includes the performance of Investor Class shares for the period prior to Institutional Class inception. Expenses have not been adjusted to reflect the expenses allocable to Institutional Class shares. If such expenses were reflected, the returns would be higher than those shown.

Performance

• The Impax Sustainable Allocation Fund (SAF) underperformed its blended benchmark (60% S&P 500/40% Bloomberg Barclays US Aggregate Bond Index).

Market overview

• Global equities, as measured by the MSCI ACWI, declined in Q1 after early strength turned negative as uncertainty surrounding US tariffs dominated sentiment and news flow. The broadening of markets drove a change in regional and sector-level leadership with US equities underperforming Chinese and European counterparts, and technology stocks lagging all sectors during the period. Following strong returns in January, US President Donald Trump spooked markets with threats, retractions, and the eventual implementation of tariffs on key trading partners. In Europe, a range of new fiscal commitments – including a EUR500bn infrastructure spending plan from Germany – led markets to outperform the US although still ending lower. In China, manufacturing activity expanded at its fastest pace in a year in March, suggesting that the government’s stimulus measures were aiding the economic recovery despite the threat of US tariffs raising growth concerns. From a sector perspective, Energy was at the forefront of index returns as global oil prices rallied. Utilities and other defensive sectors also showed relative strength during the period. The pullback in technology continued as the sector delivered the weakest returns of the quarter followed by Consumer Discretionary stocks.

• At the start of the first quarter of 2025, investors were bullish on the economy with a strong risk appetite. Optimism surrounding President Trump’s tax cuts and deregulation proposals soon gave way to concerns over his sweeping tariff proposals and their impacts on growth and inflation. The US Federal Reserve met twice during the period and held rates steady, but the release of the January Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meeting minutes suggested the Fed remained concerned about inflation, potential effects of looming tariffs, and uncertainty in the economic outlook. Despite inflation concerns, US Treasury yields fell across the board, with the longest maturities seeing the smallest declines.

Investment-grade corporate issuance rebounded during the period, with substantial new issuance coming to market. Interest rate futures priced in multiple rate cuts throughout the year, and while interest rate volatility broadly declined, it spiked mid-period before settling towards the end.

Asset allocation

• The portfolio maintains a balanced exposure between equities and fixed income, represented by a 60/40 stock-bond mix, and a 50/10 split between US and non-US stocks. The portfolio had a slight overweight in equity and an underweight in bonds and cash compared to the asset allocation benchmark. Allocation was additive as the underweight to US relative to the policy benchmark added value, as investors rotated away from US securities towards international stocks and bonds amid heighted volatility as a result of uncertainty around the new administration’s approach to global trade.

Selection – Equity

• The key driver of underperformance in the quarter was manager selection. The US core equity sleeve had the largest impact, explaining roughly two thirds of the underperformance in the period as Technology exposure sold off as investors raised concerns about how the “DeepSeek” moment and a tariff related slowdown could impact the pace of data center build outs and AI spend. The portfolio saw benefits from more defensively positioned exposure in the International and Global Sustainable Infrastructure equity sleeves provided some respite as these portfolios saw positive absolute returns in a down-market environment.

• Using sector-based performance analysis, the underperformance was primarily attributed to weaker stock selection, as sector allocation effects added value in the quarter. Sector positioning was most beneficial in Consumer Discretionary where the portfolio is underweight. The overweight to Health Care which tends to act defensively in periods of uncertainty was also additive. The portfolio’s lack of energy exposure given the manager’s fossil fuel policy was a headwind, as energy stocks rallied in the first quarter of 2025.

• Stock selection was most effective in Consumer Discretionary primarily due to not holding Tesla and an underweight to Amazon (AMZN), as well as within Real Estate and Utilities. The more tariff exposed areas of Health Care, Information Technology and Industrials which have attractive secular growth exposure to sustainability struggled amid macro uncertainty and cyclical risks.

Selection – Fixed income

• The Bond allocation underperformed its benchmark during the quarter. Allocation decisions and security selection both detracted from performance over the period. The allocation to Securitized Products benefitted performance over the quarter. Security selection among corporates also contributed, most notably within the financial services sector. Esoteric ABS security selection and positioning among US Treasuries bolstered returns.

• Security selection among the strategy’s Securitized Products and Government-Related holdings were the largest detractors from performance over the period. The strategy’s Government-related exposure is typically shorter-dated and underperformed the rally in US Treasuries. From an allocation perspective, the strategy’s exposure to esoteric ABS and off benchmark non-agency MBS, while underweight US Treasuries, also weighed on performance. Given the spread widening environment experienced during the period, the overweight allocation to credit detracted as well.

Outlook

• Looking beyond the short-term volatility triggered by Trump’s tariffs, we believe global growth remains on track to improve in 2026 after a slowdown in 2025. If the tariff negotiations last for several months, we would expect companies to start guiding for lower earnings due to a potential slowdown in Gross Domestic Product (GDP) growth. We would also expect the Fed to move to avoid a potential recession by cutting rates more than the two 25-bps reductions currently expected by the market. As long-term investors, we aim to manage portfolios through this challenging period with near-term caution and optimism for the longer term.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: 2 Picks To Make Your Money Earn More Money
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly.

Earning money by lending money out to others and collecting your money back along with interest is one of the oldest, time-tested ways of generating additional income and wealth.

If you have excess capital and someone else needs capital and is willing to pay you back the money you gave them plus extra for the hassle of having to use your money to do it, you can generate tremendous wealth over the long run. This is one reason why financial institutions have been so successful. They use other people's money to lend to a 3rd party and collect the difference while paying a small amount of interest to the initial depositor.

Various types of lending arrangements exist all over the market, but none are more well-known than mortgage loans. Most individuals need to obtain a mortgage to afford a home, and therefore, mortgage loans are considered low-risk and ubiquitous across the entire market.

Today, I want to look at opportunities to make money off of people who lend money.

Let's dive in!

Pick #1: NLY – Yield 14.5%

To say that the future of U.S. trade policy is "uncertain" is an understatement. Every week, we are bombarded with news stories of new tariff threats, tariffs being enacted, tariffs being repealed, tariffs being paused, courts blocking tariffs, and other courts blocking the blocking of the tariffs.

This uncertainty is likely the primary driver behind the 30-year U.S. Treasury Bond (US30Y) rising to 20-year highs after the initial enthusiasm that interest rates might be coming down:

The bond markets are trying to predict when the Fed will cut rates and how changing trade dynamics will impact economic output.

When long-term Treasuries are so high, it isn't a surprise that the 30-year mortgage rate is high as well. In fact, where the 30-year Treasury is about where it was 20 years ago, the 30-year mortgage rate is a good bit higher:

With all debt, a higher yield means lower prices. The "mortgage rate" is determined by the prices of agency MBS. These are mortgage-backed securities that are issued and guaranteed by the "agencies" Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac. Originators will look at the market to determine the appropriate rate to offer customers. When a person "locks in" their mortgage rate, the originator has taken a short position in MBS, guaranteeing they can sell the mortgage at the agreed-upon rate even if interest rates change before closing.

Here is a look at the prices of 5.5% coupon MBS over the past ten years: Source.

Note that this is the current coupon, and it's lower than the average mortgage rate because investors receive the 5.5%. The rate paid by the borrower is higher because several other entities are getting a piece, including the "servicer" which is the company that actually receives the payment from the borrower, engages in collection efforts if necessary, and passes along the money to the various parties that own pieces of the loan.

Annaly Capital Management, Inc. (NLY) participates in several areas of the mortgage market, including agency MBS, non-agency mortgages, and MSRs (Mortgage Servicing Rights). Source.

Agency MBS makes up $75 billion in assets because this business strategy is conducive to using leverage. Note that this segment has $75 billion in assets with $8 billion in capital for leverage of 9.37x debt/equity. Meanwhile, the non-agency mortgages are leveraged at 2.75x, and the MSRs are leveraged at 1.22x.

The net result is that what happens in the agency MBS segment has an overwhelming impact on NLY's book value and earnings. That's why we spend so much time talking about it when we cover NLY. Annaly's book value is near multi-decade lows because the price of MBS is near multi-decade lows. And, as an asset with minimal credit risk, agency MBS is highly correlated with U.S. Treasury prices.

The agency MBS strategy relies on borrowing using repurchase agreements or "repos". These are very short-term borrowing agreements, typically less than 90 days, where the agency MBS is used as collateral. Repos are available at very low interest rates, typically just a small spread of the overnight rate that the Fed manipulates with their "target rate."

In Q1, NLY paid an average of 4.56%, with an average maturity of 50 days: Source.

NLY profits on the difference between the coupon it is collecting from the agency MBS, and the price it is paying for leverage. Note that in Q1, NLY's actual interest expense was lower due to holding interest rate swaps that reduced interest expense to an effective 3.88%.

NLY has been taking advantage of lower MBS prices by buying up higher coupons, with 77% of its portfolio at 4.5% or higher:

As the Fed raised interest rates, it created an "inverted yield curve" where long-term rates were lower than short-term rates. This business strategy, is uncomfortable because it relies on being able to borrow at a lower rate than received on the mortgages. NLY's interest rate swaps helped keep realized interest expense below interest income. Today, NLY is at a point where its average coupon is higher than its interest expense. This is what drove NLY to decide to raise its dividend in Q1, and the longer it has to buy higher coupon MBS, the better the outlook for future dividend raises.

The ideal, for NLY, would be a scenario where the Fed cuts and mortgage rates remain at 7%. However, it is likely that long-term rates will come down a bit if the Fed resumes its cutting cycle.

We expect that NLY will continue to take advantage of the buying opportunity in agency MBS. Someday, mortgage rates are going to come back down to Earth; the longer it takes, the more NLY can rotate its portfolio to the higher current yields.

The current drama being created in the long-term Treasury markets is a buying opportunity for a lot of different types of debt. Agency MBS has one of the most attractive risk/reward dynamics. The big risk with this strategy is the inverted yield curve, and NLY has usually seen its share price bottom out during a yield curve inversion. They hedge to remain cash-flow positive, but when borrowing expenses are higher than the coupon being received, the cash flow trend is going to be downward as hedges mature.

Today, that danger is in the rearview mirror as current coupon MBS are higher than current interest expense, and while the Fed is cutting a lot slower than some thought, the discussion is still hold or cut, instead of hold or raise. Another inversion is a risk, but an unlikely one in our opinion. Book value recovery will depend on MBS prices and U.S. Treasury prices, and can't be easily predicted. Cash flow is recovering, and that trend is well underway. We saw NLY's first dividend raise of the cycle, and we expect we will see several more over the next few years.

Pick #2: AGM-G – Yield 6.7%

While Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac have been making headlines, Farmer Mac, also known as Federal Agricultural Mortgage Corporation (AGM) has been quietly ignored for decades. AGM plays an essential role in funding rural development, whether financing rural infrastructure or enabling farmers to expand their personal holdings with the massive liquidity that's needed to operate those facilities and purchase the land or equipment. AGM itself does not support an attractive dividend yield at 3%, but its earnings and size enable it to afford extremely low-cost debt. One of the low-cost debt options that they have chosen to do is to issue preferred securities with extremely low yields at PAR.

What makes this attractive for an income investor is that many of these extremely low-yield-at-par securities are trading at large discounts because the yields on these preferred securities need to be higher than treasury yields for them to be attractive to new buyers. So there are opportunities to buy a qualified dividend income-paying security at massive discounts, offering yields within the 6-7% range with exceptionally low call risk because it's unlikely that the AGM will be able to issue out a new preferred security with a lower yield in the future. One such security is Federal Agricultural Mortgage Corp. 4.875% Non-Cumulative Preferred Stock Series G (AGM.PR.G), currently sporting a yield of 6.7%.

Conclusion

NLY and AGM-G are two different ways to collect wonderful income from companies that make money by lending money. In NLY's case, you are collecting interest and principal with every mortgage payment that homeowners make to afford their homes and build their equity. With AGM, you're able to collect income from providing capital that AGM then uses to lend out in their lending process to rural development. In both cases, your money is rapidly earning for you over the long run. Both options enable you to enjoy strong cash flow, but they offer very different risk-reward profiles. One offers you a much higher yield that typically benefits when the economy is struggling, while the other offers a lower QDI yield but does not offer as much potential upside. Together, they provide you with a two-pronged method of collecting great income from the lending activity that companies make to support the development or the enablement of homeownership.

When it comes to retirement, finding sources to collect income is wonderful. There's nothing I enjoy more than tapping into a new income source and sitting back and watching those dividend dollars pour into my account, providing me with the income I need to live day-to-day, reinvest back in the market, or explore other opportunities. These two opportunities presented today provide you with the chance to do just that. You can find these, along with nearly 100 others, within our model portfolio, which High Dividend Opportunities members have access to.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

the returns would be higher than those shown. Institutional Class shares’ average annual return since June 1, 2004 is 5.21% (annualized). The performance information shown for Class A represents the performance of the Investor Class shares for the period prior to Class A inception. Expenses have not been adjusted to reflect the expenses allocable to Class A shares. Class A inception date return since May 1, 2013 is 3.29% (annualized). A 1.00% CDSC (contingent deferred sales charge) may be charged on any shares sold within 18 months of purchase over $1 million. POP (public offering price) reflects the maximum sales load for the Fund’s Class A Shares of 4.50%.

Credit ratings are divided into categories ranging from AAA (highest) to D (lowest). The ratings shown are determined by using the middle rating of available Nationally Recognized Statistical Rating Organizations (NRSROs). When a rating from only two organizations is available, the lower rating is used. When a rating from only one is available, that rating is used. When a security is not rated by one of these organizations, it is designated as Not Rated. Securities designated as Not Rated do not necessarily indicate low credit quality. Holdings of the portfolio other than bonds are categorized as Other. Credit ratings apply to the underlying holdings of the portfolio and not the portfolio itself, are subjective opinions of NRSRO, may become stale and are subject to change.

Performance

• The Impax High Yield Bond Fund outperformed its benchmark on over the first quarter. Although security selection detracted, this was offset by positive allocation decisions.

Market review

• During the first quarter of 2025, the high yield bond market experienced significant volatility. Investors were initially bullish on the economy with a moderately strong risk appetite. However, the optimism surrounding US President Donald Trump’s tax cuts and deregulation proposals soon gave way to concerns over his sweeping tariffs and their impacts on growth and inflation. Against this backdrop, the strategy’s benchmark, the ICE BofA BB-B US Cash Pay High Yield Constrained Index, was positive the quarter. The US Federal Reserve (Fed) met twice during the period and held rates steady at each meeting.

However, the Fed’s March Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) statement expressed increased uncertainty in the economic outlook. Although the updated ‘dot plot’ suggested the Fed would stay the course through 2025 and 2026 with just two 25 basis point (BPs) cuts in each of the years, the March Summary of Economic Projections revised 2025-2027 GDP growth downward while raising unemployment and core Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) projections for 2025. US Treasury yields fell across the board on the increased uncertainty and prospects of a slowdown in economic growth with the 2-, 5-, and 10-year segments falling 36 bps, 43 bps, and 37 bps, respectively. The spread tightening seen early during the period quickly gave way to a steady widening after the mid-February release of the January FOMC meetings suggested the Fed remained concerned about inflation, the potential knock-on effects of looming tariffs, and uncertainty in the economic outlook. Towards the end of the period, spreads rapidly rose on increased tariff rhetoric from President Trump. The yield-to- worst on the benchmark rose by 13 bps while the par-weighted coupon fell modestly by 5 bps to end the period at 6.93% and 6.25%, respectively.

Market review

• During the period, performance across the high yield market was driven by the higher-quality segments, with BBs1 and Single Bs2 posting positive returns and outperforming the CCC and below3 segment which posted a negative return. From a sector perspective, the Banking, Real Estate, and Insurance sectors were the top performers. Conversely, the Transportation, Technology & Electronics, and Services sectors were the worst performers over the quarter.

• The par-weighted high yield bond default rate4, including distressed exchanges, continued to decrease to multi-year lows and well below the 25-year average. Leveraged loan default rates also decreased over the quarter. The decreases in both rates were driven by the lightest quarterly distressed exchange volume since the fourth quarter of 2022.

• The US$87.4bn of investor demand, including US$7.6bn of mutual fund inflows, matched the available supply which was split across US$68.3bn in gross new issuance and US$19.1bn in fallen angels5.

Contributors

• During the period, the overweight allocations to the Real Estate, Insurance, and Banking sectors positively contributed to performance. Security selection among the Real Estate, Telecommunications, and Capital Goods sectors also benefitted performance. Notable companies within these sectors include Medical Properties Trust (MPW), Regal Rexnord (RRX), and Viasat (VSAT). Regal Rexnord’s bonds generally rose throughout the quarter, despite lower-than-expected fourth quarter 2024 earnings. This occurred after management highlighted improving orders momentum relative to the prior year, as well as a negligible anticipated impact from tariffs. Viasat reported strong fourth quarter 2024 results and announced that the company had signed additional contracts with the US Department of Defense and several airlines which have calmed some of the fears of Starlink’s (STRLK) disruption of the industry. The bonds of Medical Properties Trust, were called at par.

Detractors

• The overweight allocations to the Technology & Electronics and Transportation sectors detracted over the quarter. The strategy’s lack of exposure to the Energy sector modestly detracted. Security selection within the Retail sector was the largest detractor to performance over the quarter. Security selection within the Automotive and Services sectors was also challenged but to a lesser extent. Notable companies within these sectors include Walgreens Boots Alliance (WBA), Adient (ADNT), and Staples. The strategy did not own the Walgreens bonds when they traded up on the speculation that they might be redeemed after the company announced its leveraged buy-out by Sycamore. Staples’ bonds traded down due to the risk-off sentiment of the market driven by tariff news and general concerns about consumer sentiment. However, we maintain a strong conviction in this name over the long term. The bonds of Adient, an automobile parts manufacturer, similarly traded down on tariff concerns.

Outlook

• Credit spreads widened during the quarter, reflecting a significant rise in uncertainty across the global economy. Between unresolved tariff issues, weakening US economic data, and stalled progress between Ukraine and Russia, market participants have become more cautious. Lower-quality credit has underperformed so far this year, which is in line with expectations. Our investment team has reduced risk at the margins and remains ready to take further defensive steps if necessary.

• Although spreads have sold off sharply in recent weeks, they remain only modestly above their 10-year average and well below levels typically seen in past recessions. Compared to other recent periods of market anxiety – such as the growth concerns of October 2023 and the recession fears of 2022 – spreads are still relatively tight. Importantly, credit markets are not yet pricing in a recession.

• The outlook will largely depend on the outcome of tariff negotiations, companies’ ability to pass through higher costs, and the continued strength of the US labour market. If tariffs are resolved successfully, corporate fundamentals could remain stable, and a relief rally may follow given the current attractive yields. On the other hand, a breakdown in negotiations would sharply raise recession risks, likely resulting in wider spreads, higher yields, and increased defaults. For now, recession concerns remain lower than they were in 2023.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Markets Face A New Reality As The Era Of Artificially Low Rates Ends
data.description:
It is a big week of central bank policy meetings with the Bank of Japan up first, followed by the Federal Reserve, and concludes with the Bank of England. All three central banks face similar problems of rising long-term interest rates. The jury is out on why rates on 30-year rates are rising, but the most likely answer may also be the most apparent: monetary policy is no longer suppressing rates, and price discovery is taking place. This is a new reality that the market has not yet entirely accepted.

Yield Curve Suppression

Since 2008, central banks have gone to great lengths to support markets and economies with aggressive forms of monetary policy. Zero and Negative interest rate policy became the norm, along with massive quantitative easing programs, and in Japan's case, even yield curve control. It was, as Mario Draghi once said, a "whatever-it-takes" period, and the Fed, ECB, BOE, and BOJ didn't hold back.

Not only did COVID-19 usher in a period of ultra-easy monetary policy, but it also brought vast amounts of stimulus and fiscal spending, something that was notably lacking following the recovery up until COVID-19. However, it also means that bond yields were artificially suppressed, and now, for the first time in more than a decade, these programs are gone, and the bond markets are repricing.

But more importantly, it is that easy policy and vast sums of quantitative easing, along with big fiscal spending, brought back inflation. With inflation, central banks were forced to raise interest rates, pushing them off the effective zero bound. With rates in the EU, the UK, and the US now firmly above the zero bound, we are seeing interest rates reprice across the yield curve.

While this week's meetings are unlikely to bring about any policy changes, it is likely to remind markets once again that we have moved beyond the days of easy policy and quantitative easing, and that we aren't expected to return to those conditions anytime soon.

Policy Rates Are Nearing Their Destination

The ECB reminds us of this as its overnight rate settles in at 2%, and at its most recent meeting signals that the end of the rate-cutting cycle may be near, and more importantly, the destination is nowhere close to the lows witnessed over the past decade.

Common sense would seem to suggest that if the Fed's overnight borrowing rate is going to settle out at 3.25% as the Fed Funds market predicts, why shouldn't a 30-year Treasury be trading at 5% or higher; it would seem to make sense that investors would demand a higher premium for owning long-term debt. This same logic applies to the other curves as well, and based on current spreads, long-end rates have further to steepen compared to policy rates.

We Were Living In The Outlier Years

Another way to think about this is to consider an interest rate on a UK Consol Bond dating back to 1750. Since the data for these Consol Bonds ends around 2020, the UK 30-year Rate has been used to continue the series. The chart clearly shows that 30-year rates in the UK today are back to the historical norm before the volatile 1970s and the depressed levels of the 2010s. Never before in 300 years had interest rates been as depressed as they were following the financial crisis, and that includes some pretty tough times in the first half of the 20th century. It appears from examining this chart that the outliers were the 1970s and 1980s, as well as the period from 2008 to 2022.

With this in mind, it makes complete sense why long-end rates are rising and why they may continue to rise. If monetary policy is going to leave overnight rates higher, well above the trends of the past decade, and more in line with historical norms, then it seems only natural for long-end rates to follow suit. The only reason it may be happening now, as opposed to two or three years ago, is that we now have a better understanding of where monetary policy may ultimately settle.

Of course, the big unknown is whether recession throws this off course, which is always the big worry. However, even a mild recession is unlikely to alter the policy path significantly, and certainly wouldn't warrant an aggressive policy response. It would take an event like the 2008 financial crisis or a COVID-19 pandemic to elicit the type of policy response that brings rates back to zero and triggers quantitative easing. As of right now, that doesn't appear to be on the horizon.

data.title: D-Wave Quantum: I Wouldn't Gamble My Money On Such Massive Speculation
data.description:
Quantum Computing: Commercialization Soon?

The quantum computing leaders are getting a lot of attention recently, as we seem to be moving closer to actualizing commercialization of the nascent technology, making a transformative narrative shift from pure speculation to full-scale monetization. And for D-Wave Quantum Inc. (NYSE:QBTS), the enthusiasm couldn't have been more pronounced as the stock made a new recent high in May 2025, coming off a relatively upbeat Q1 earnings release.

Notably, the quantum computing company seeks to justify the "quantum advantage" of its annealing architecture (vis-à-vis the gate model approach), with a successful $12.6M sale of the Advantage2 annealing quantum computer to the Julich Supercomputer Center. I believe it has arguably assured investors that its monetization options have broadened beyond just cloud services, auguring well as hardware sales are noted to have a markedly higher gross margin profile.

In my previous QBTS article, I exhorted investors to stay "sane," and avoid doubling down on the stock, while waiting for the bubble to implode first. While the thesis panned out as the stock fell more than 50% from its March zenith through its April lows, it has already recovered all those losses and more, surging to a new 2025 peak, and outperforming the S&P 500 (SPX) (SP500) and peers represented in the Defiance Quantum ETF (QTUM).

As the leading quantum constituent in the ETF, the outperformance has also lifted QTUM, spurring it toward the highs last visited in late 2024. Yet, I also think it's opportune for us to ascertain whether the recent bullish sentiments and solid outperformance should be taken as an encouragement for investors to double down on their holdings, or contemplate leveraging the bullishness to size out their allocation.

QBTS: Moving Into Hardware Sales

I think the market has undoubtedly caught on to the positive operating performance trends that underpinned the recent buying fervor. As the company broadened its revenue levers, it has also helped justify a significant improvement in its adjusted EBITDA losses. Total customer count has also been lifted, although I assess that tracking for near-term profitability isn't expected in the near term as QBTS aims to broaden its adoption across more commercial and enterprise customers.

Despite that, Wall Street analysts have bolstered their near-term revenue and earnings outlook on QBTS, although D-Wave Quantum is still not expected to deliver FCF profitability through 2026. While the company has astutely solidified its liquidity position through the recent buying surge with a $400M stock sales agreement, bearish sentiments on the stock climbed through the end of May, with QBTS's short interest ratio reaching almost 20% of its outstanding float.

QBTS: Jensen Huang Sees An "Inflection Point"

Yet, Nvidia (NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang has arguably kept buying enthusiasm on QBTS and its quantum peers at a relatively high-level following the "change of heart" from his less sanguine views from early 2025. Huang underscored his belief at a recent conference that quantum computing appears to be at the cusp of a noteworthy "inflection point," aligning with the confidence of IBM (IBM), as it seeks to build a large scale fault-tolerant quantum computer by 2029.

Yet, we must also take into account that it remains uncertain whether such a momentum can be sustained through the rest of the year, given the significant 84% accounted by the hardware sales notched in Q1. We must consider these lumpy opportunities carefully, and I don't think it's reasonable for us to construe it as something that could be replicated quarter after quarter consistently. At these early stages of the monetization phase, we must be prepared for substantive sales cycle volatility, particularly since the company also reported a steep 64% reduction in bookings for Q1 as compared to the same period last year.

Furthermore, it remains to be seen whether D-Wave Quantum's architecture could prove superior to the more typical gate model approach adopted by some of its leading peers. Management indicated that it has also led to some friction with government customers, plausibly also tempering future sales cadence.

In addition, concerns about whether near-term monetization can really pan out, and how soon is this "inflection point" we are talking about could linger. Hence, a healthy dose of skepticism is conceivably justified to ensure that we don't go FOMO on a speculative stock, while fending off several competitors (including big tech) as they aim to define the leading standard in quantum computing.

Buy Or Sell QBTS Stock?

Now, let me be clear about this. I must say that I've been very impressed by the incredibly resilient QBTS stock that has managed to overcome the affliction that saw it collapse more than 50% through its April nadir.

As a result, it has helped to keep the bullish bias on the stock, with its robust momentum likely adding on to the losses that have engulfed its stubborn short sellers.

QBTS appears to have managed to break decisively above the $12 level, which is assessed to be a resistance zone that previously denied buying advances fervently. However, with the NTM EV/Revenue multiple surging well above the 300x mark this week (based on S&P Cap IQ data), questions must be asked whether such optimism is justified.

I believe investors hanging on to the stock must be prepared for significant volatility as seen in April. In addition, there's no guarantee that QBTS's architecture can emerge as the dominant one over its peers, notwithstanding recent progress.

IBM's commentary indicates that optimism about having a fault-tolerant quantum computer is still five years out, while Jensen Huang also didn't articulate a clear timeframe for us to focus on. QBTS's management has also not promulgated additional clarity on its profitability trajectory, suggesting that its revenue levers are expected to remain highly volatile and dependent on adoption momentum, while also being susceptible to lumpy sales cadence.

With that in mind, I'm not convinced that I alter my bearish rating on QBTS, and investors should seriously consider lowering or even fully take their exposure off.

Rating: Maintain Sell.

data.title: AI To Propel Snowflake Stock Higher
data.description:
Snowflake (NYSE:SNOW) is finally catching the AI opportunity bandwagon. The company’s recent Q1 results demonstrate that its revamped go-to-market strategy and new AI product launches are proving successful. The company’s key metrics were strong, particularly its remaining performance obligations (RPO), which grew by 34% YoY to $6.7 billion.

Management’s focus on margin expansion is gaining momentum as the adjusted operating margin improved by 440 basis points YoY in Q1. Snowflake also recently acquired Crunchy Data in a bid to tap the agentic AI opportunity. This is a further testament that the CEO, Sridhar Ramaswamy, is successfully steering the company in tapping the massive AI opportunity.

Product Momentum

During the recent Investor Day 2025, CEO Sridhar Ramaswamy reiterated the company’s focus on incorporating AI into the entire data life cycle, from data ingestion to generating predictive analytics. The company announced new products during the Snowflake Summit 2025, like the Standard Warehouse – Generation 2, which delivers faster analytics performance. It also introduced Snowflake Adaptive Compute (now in private preview), a new compute service that automatically optimizes computational resources, among others.

The company also announced its plan to buy PostgreSQL open-source database provider Crunchy Data for $250 million, which has an annual revenue of $30 million. PostgreSQL is the most popular database among developers, according to the 2024 Stack Overflow Developer Survey. The move follows its competitor Databricks' decision last month to acquire PostgreSQL company Neon for $1 billion. Snowflake aims to provide developers with the tools to build production-ready AI agents and apps.

During the Q1 earnings call, management highlighted that Cortex AI is making significant progress and is used by over 5,200 customers. Kraft Heinz (KHC) used Snowflake Cortex to build its new internal AI assistant. Similarly, Cortex Agent, launched earlier this year, is helping companies like Luminate Data gain better and faster insights from both structured and unstructured data.

Strong Revenue Growth

Snowflake is one of the fastest-growing cloud companies. The company’s revenue has grown from $264.7 million in FY2020 (ending in January 2020) to $3.6 billion in FY2025 (ending in January 2025), representing a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 68.7% during this period. Revenue is expected to reach $10.1 billion for FY2030, implying a CAGR of 22.8% in the next five years.

Snowflake’s Q1 revenue grew by 25.7% YoY to $1.04 billion, beating estimates by 3.6%. The solid revenue growth demonstrates that the company’s revamped go-to-market strategy and new AI product features are proving successful. The company is benefitting from using its own AI tools to connect with higher-value customers. It is also a win for the company's strategic focus on specific high-growth workloads, such as AI and data engineering. Analysts expect revenue to grow 25.1% and 24.1% in the next two quarters.

Product revenue grew by 26% YoY to $997 million and excluding the impact of leap year, product revenue grew by 28%. It implies that there was no deceleration from the 28% growth in Q4. The company witnessed meaningful growth from new products, particularly Snowpark and Dynamic Tables, which outperformed management expectations.

Management also raised the full-year product revenue guidance to $4.325 billion from the earlier guidance of $4.28 billion, implying a YoY growth of 25%. The company’s guidance revision by 110 basis points is quite positive, particularly in the current tough macro environment.

RPO

Q1 Remaining Performance Obligations (RPO) grew by 34% YoY to $6.7 billion, accelerating from 33% growth in Q4. The company expects 50% of the RPO to be recognized as revenue in the next one year. Snowflake was also able to close the two pending contracts of over $100 million in Q1. These two large customers in the financial services sector had previously run out of capacity in Q4.

Customers

Snowflake has been steadily growing its customer base. Total customers grew by 19% YoY to 11,578 in Q1, with the addition of 451 customers in the recent quarter. It included 754 of the Forbes Global 2000 companies, and they contributed to 41% of the total revenue in Q1. The company witnessed strong growth among its largest customers. Customers with trailing 12-month product revenue of over $1 million grew by 27% YoY to 606, adding 27 customers in the recent quarter.

NRR

The company has a solid Net Revenue Retention Rate (NRR) of 124% in Q1, even though it was down 2 percentage points sequentially. During the Q4 earnings call, management mentioned that they expect NRR in the range of mid-120s.

Margins

Snowflake’s margins are not on par with other cloud companies like CrowdStrike (CRWD). The cybersecurity company was able to achieve GAAP profitability in April 2023. The silver lining is that Snowflake management’s focus on improving margins is showing results. The adjusted operating margin improved 440 basis points YoY to 8.8% in Q1. The company’s cost-cutting initiatives included moving to centralized teams, removing redundant management layers, targeting early-career hiring, and improving productivity through the use of AI, among others.

Management also reiterated the full-year product adjusted gross margin of 75%, adjusted operating margin of 8%, and adjusted free cash flow margin of 25%. The strong adjusted operating margin in the recent quarter gives scope for revising the margin guidance upwards during this year.

Valuation

The company is trading at a P/S ratio of 18, which is below its average 3-year P/S ratio of 20.9, and has a forward P/S ratio of 15.4. The company has been trading at a premium valuation, like other cloud companies, due to the strong revenue growth.

Snowflake is trading lower than Palantir’s (NASDAQ:PLTR) forward P/S ratio of 83.2, Cloudflare’s (NYSE:NET) forward P/S ratio of 28.4, CrowdStrike’s (NASDAQ:CRWD) forward P/S ratio of 25, Zscaler’s (NASDAQ:ZS) forward P/S ratio of 17.7, and higher than Datadog’s (NASDAQ:DDOG) forward P/S ratio of 12.9.

Risks to consider

The company is not GAAP profitable. It faces competition from companies like Databricks and also from hyperscaler services like Amazon Redshift, Google BigQuery, and Microsoft Azure Synapse. The company also has a very high stock-based compensation. During the Q4 earnings call, management mentioned that stock-based compensation is expected to reduce to about 37% of revenue in FY2026 from 41% in FY2025 and will continue to decrease going forward. While the reduction is positive, it is still high.

Snowflake has a consumption-based business model. It is beneficial during times of economic growth as its customers increase consumption, thereby leading to higher revenue. On the other hand, during times of economic uncertainty, companies tend to reduce their consumption. Therefore, this business model may exhibit higher quarterly revenue volatility.

Conclusion

I have a buy rating for Snowflake, as it is well-positioned to capture the growing demand for AI with its new AI product features. The company is also moving in the right direction under the leadership of CEO Sridhar Ramaswamy. This is also evident from Snowflake’s results in the last few quarters. On the other hand, the stock is up 65% over the last year. It would be crucial for the company to set the bar high in the coming quarters, especially since the company is not GAAP profitable.

data.title: WIW: 8%-Plus Yield, 11% Discounted Inflation Protection
data.description:
With tariff wars rattling the market on a nearly daily basis, many economists have raised their inflation forecasts for 2025.

With that in mind, we've invested in Western Asset Infl-Linked Opps & Inc Fd (WIW). It's a closed-end fund, a "CEF."

Fund Profile:

WIW has a leveraged portfolio investing at least 80% in inflation-linked securities, with the ability to invest in other fixed-income assets including high-yield, emerging markets, structured products, commodities, and currency. The fund seeks current income, with a secondary investment objective of capital appreciation.

WIW began on 2/27/2004. It has $592M in Net Assets, with 144 holdings, and 229K in average daily volume. Management uses ~31% leverage, so the expense ratio is higher than usual, at 3.57%, as there's 2.87% in interest expense:

Holdings:

The portfolio is primarily investment grade, with 85% in AA securities, 2.11% in AAA, 2.11% in A, and 4.92% in BBB.

The U.S. is by far the biggest exposure, at 129.54%, very distantly followed by Brazil, at 2.98%, and Mexico, at 1.94%, with less than 1% in several other nations:

3-5 year maturities are the largest exposure, at 37.26%, but there are many other maturities: 1–3 years, at 21.89%, and 7–10 years, at 15.51%, with 14.5% in 15–20 years, and 11.12% in 20–30 years.

The portfolio's duration is 7.54 years.

As of 3/31/25, there were 5 main types of securities, with the main one being Inflation-Linked, at 82.59%. WIW also has 4.8% in Emerging Market debt, 4.66% in Investment Grade Credit, plus ~3% each in Non-Agency Mortgage Backed Securities and Commercial Mortgage Backed Securities. There were several other types of securities held, with less than 1% exposure:

The U.S. inflation-linked securities have maturities running from 2025 out to 2045, with the 2029 holding the largest, at 20.48%, and the 01/26 one at 15.55%.

Dividends:

At its 6/13/25 intraday price of $8.55, WIW yielded 8.77%. It pays monthly, and goes ex-dividend next on 6/23/25, with a 6/30/25 pay date.

WIW's base dividend/share grew by an average of ~13.76% over the past 5 years. Its total annual dividends grew by an average of 38.75%, but that figure is distorted by a big 262.85% jump in 2021, due to the $.95 in special distributions that year.

NII declined from $21.8M in 2023 to $17.3M in 2024, while Net Realized Losses improved from $-6.33M in 2023 to $2.4M in 2024. Net Unrealized Appreciation swung from a $14.42M loss in 2023, to a $20.71M gain in 2024.

WIW had an 8% decline in assets in 2023, but a smaller 1.5% decline in 2024, ending the year at $606.9M, The fiscal year ends on November 30th:

Taxes:

Mgt. estimates that NII accounted for 100% of the monthly distributions for December '24 - February '25:

For fiscal year 2024, NII was also estimated to comprise 100% of WIW's distributions:

Performance:

As of 3/31/25, WIW outperformed the Bloomberg US Government Inflation-Linked 1-10 year index on a price basis by a wide margin over the past year, at ~11% vs. 6% for the index.

It also outperformed the index over the past 5- and 10-year periods, while underperforming it somewhat since inception.

It underperformed it on an NAV basis over the past 1-year and 3-year periods, and outperformed it over the past 5-year period. It was even with the index over the past 10 years, and a bit lower since inception:

So far in 2025, WIW is up 3.45% in price, vs. 2.61% for the S&P 500.

Risks:

Fixed income securities involve interest rate, credit, inflation and reinvestment risks, and possible loss of principal. As interest rates rise, the value of fixed income securities falls. Low-rated, high-yield bonds are subject to greater price volatility, illiquidity and the possibility of default.

Liquidity risk exists when securities or other investments become more difficult to sell, or are unable to be sold, at the price at which they have been valued.

International investments are subject to special risks, including currency fluctuations and social, economic, and political uncertainties, which could increase volatility. These risks are magnified in emerging markets.

Currency management strategies could result in losses to the fund if currencies do not perform as expected.

Valuations:

Buying CEF's at deeper than historic discounts to NAV can be a useful strategy, due to mean reversion. CEFs' daily valuations are calculated at the end of each trading day.

As of its 6/12/25 close, WIW was selling at an 11.46% discount to NAV/share.

Parting Thoughts:

As of June 13, 2025, WIW was ~4% above its 52-week low. While its 11.46% discount isn't quite as deep as its 12.26% 5-year average discount, it's close enough to attract us as buyers, given its ability to hedge inflation. We rate WIW a Buy.

All tables furnished by Hidden Dividend Stocks Plus, unless otherwise

data.title: Impax Core Bond Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance

• The Impax Core Bond Fund underperformed its benchmark during the first quarter. Allocation decisions and security selection both detracted from performance over the period.

Performance (%) Total return Average annual return
1M 3M YTD 1Y 3Y 5Y 10Y SI1
Institutional Class -0.12 2.59 2.59 4.69 0.51 -0.22 - 1.42
Investor Class -0.14 2.41 2.41 4.31 0.26 -0.46 - 1.17
Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond 0.04 2.78 2.78 4.88 0.52 -0.4 - 1.58

Market overview

• At the start of the first quarter of 2025 investors were bullish on the economy with a moderately strong risk appetite. However, the optimism surrounding US President Trump’s tax cuts and deregulation proposals soon gave way to concerns over his sweeping tariff proposals and their impacts on growth and inflation. Against this macroeconomic backdrop, the investment grade market1 outperformed high yield2. The US Federal Reserve (Fed) met twice during the period and held rates steady at each meeting. However, the release of the January Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meeting minutes in mid-February added to market volatility as they suggested the Fed remained concerned about inflation, the potential knock- on effects of looming tariffs, and uncertainty in the economic outlook. The March FOMC statement reinforced the Fed’s concerns noting that the uncertainty had increased since their prior meeting. The ‘dot plot’, released in mid-March, remained unchanged and suggested that the median Fed member still projected two 25-basis point (BPs) cuts during 2025 and two additional cuts in 2026. However, the March Summary of Economic Projections reinforced the Fed’s cautious view of the economy as 2025 GDP growth was revised downward by 0.4% to 1.7%; 2026 and 2027 growth projections showed smaller drops while longer-run projections remained unchanged. Upward revisions to the unemployment rate and core PCE inflation were front-loaded to 2025 while projections for 2026 remained unchanged. At the end of the period, interest rate futures were pricing in three 25 bps cuts in 2025. Interest rate volatility3 broadly declined during the first half of the period, with the ICE BofA MOVE index falling from 99 to 84, only to rise to an intra-period high of 115 before closing out the period at 101.

• Despite inflation concerns being fueled by proposed tariffs, their effect on growth caused US Treasury yields to fall across the board. The 2- and 10-year segments fell by 36 and 37 bps, respectively, while the 30-year segment fell by 21 bps.

• Although the option-adjusted spread on the benchmark initially fell during the first half of the period it rose sharply during the latter half and ended the quarter only modestly higher. All sectors posted positive total returns over the quarter, with the securitised sector leading the way while corporates lagged. However, excess returns were negative across most sectors as spreads broadly widened during the period, most notably in long duration corporates. Agency MBS was the lone sector that experienced spread compression.

• Investment grade corporate issuance bounced back during the period with US$482.4bn in gross and US$225.9bn in net new issuance coming to market.4

Contributors

The allocation to Securitised Products benefited performance over the quarter. Security selection among Corporates also contributed, most notably within the Financial Services sector. Esoteric ABS security selection and positioning among US Treasuries bolstered returns.

Detractors

Security selection among the strategy’s Securitized Products and Government-Related holdings were the largest detractors from performance over the period. The strategy’s Government-related exposure is typically shorter-dated and underperformed the rally in US Treasuries. From an allocation perspective, the strategy’s exposure to esoteric ABS and off benchmark non-agency MBS, while underweight US Treasuries, also weighed on performance. Given the spread widening environment experienced during the period, the overweight allocation to credit detracted as well.

Outlook

Markets have rapidly shifted from a risk-on to a risk-off tone amid a flurry of economic and geopolitical proposals from the new administration. Many of the proposals were announced – and in some cases reversed – at a swift pace, creating a highly fluid outlook. The investment team believes the US Federal Reserve is expected to remain patient, holding rates steady as it assesses the impact of these evolving policies. Recession risks are rising, with the investment team’s probability increasing from 30% to 40%, with the likelihood of a soft landing now estimated at just 30 – 50%. In early April, a 90-day reprieve on most reciprocal tariffs was announced which provides hope that if there is a recession, it is likely to be shallow. The risk of a stagflationary environment has also risen. Despite tariff-driven inflation concerns, the broad fall in US Treasury yields reflects a market focused on recession risks. However, after initially falling, long-end US Treasury yields have recently begun rapidly rising. The catalyst for this rise appears to be a combination of inflation risk, hedge fund deleveraging, and a projected increase in the fiscal deficit. Consumer fundamentals remain relatively stable, however, both prime and subprime delinquencies are climbing, and sentiment has deteriorated to recessionary levels. Given the rapidly shifting landscape and mounting risks, the investment team believes it is prudent to reduce credit exposure across both corporates and structured products and reallocate to towards the US rates market.

Footnotes

  1. Bloomberg US Aggregate Index, March 2025
  2. ICE BofA US High Yield Cash Pay Constrained Index, March 2025
  3. ICE BofA MOVE Index, March 20253
  4. Barclays US Investment Grade Corporate Update, March 2025

Performance data quoted represent past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For most recent month-end performance information, visit www.impaxam.com. Figures include reinvested dividends, capital gains distribution, and changes in principal value.

1First full month since inception The inception date for Institutional Class is 12/16/2016 and Investor Class is 12/16/2016. As of 05/01/2024 prospectus expense ratio for Institutional Class and Investor Class shares are 0.47%, 0.72%, respectively.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Shopify's Dip Buying Opportunity May Be Coming
data.description:
SHOP's Rally Has Occurred As Per Historical Trends, With A Dip Buying Opportunity Likely In The Near-Term

We previously covered Shopify Inc. (NYSE:SHOP) (TSX:SHOP:CA) in February 2025, discussing how its unified solutions across the commerce (offline and online)/ payment ecosystem had continued to drive the growth flywheel and the cross-selling opportunities.

Combined with the higher commerce/ fintech penetration, the expanding profit margins, the Rule of 50 outperformance at 53x in FY2024, and the moderating short interest volumes, we had upgraded the stock to a Buy upon a correction then.

SHOP 1Y Stock Price

Since then, SHOP has indeed retraced deeply during the worst of the April 2025 market wide meltdown, with the stock breaching our prior fair value estimates of $87.20 to bounce at the $76s floor, before rapidly recovering to prior February 2025 heights.

Much of the recovery tailwinds are attributed to the 90-day tariff pause and the ongoing de-escalation in the trade war between the US and China, with it underscoring why sentiments surrounding consumer discretionary/ commerce stocks have lifted, despite the slowing consumer spending trends.

A similar exuberance has been observed in other enterprise SaaS stocks as well, with it apparent that demand remains robust no matter the uncertainties arising from the macroeconomic headwinds and the tariff risks, as similarly reported by numerous market players in the recent Q1'25 earnings season, including Microsoft (MSFT) and Adobe (ADBE).

The same has been reported by SHOP, with FQ1'25 revenues of $2.36B (-16% QoQ/ +28.6% YoY), richer adj operating margins of 13.9% (-6.9 points QoQ/ +3.1 YoY/ +5.6 from FY2019 levels of 3%), and adj EPS of $0.25 (-43.1% QoQ/ +25% YoY).

Most importantly, the commerce SaaS company has continued to report a growing Gross Merchandise Volume [GMV] of $74.75B (-20.8% QoQ/ +22.8% YoY) and Monthly Recurring Revenue [MRR] of $182M (-3.1% QoQ/ +20.5% YoY), with it underscoring the robust adoption of its merchant and fintech offerings.

This development is unsurprising indeed, given SHOP's undeniable market leadership in the headless commerce segment with 28% in the US market share, significantly aided by the team's agility in responding to the rapidly developing global trade cadence.

The same has been observed in their ability to launch numerous tariff related features within weeks of the announcement, across filter features, duties calculations at checkout, and tariffguide.ai for merchants looking to source for cheaper suppliers, amongst others.

This is on top of the expanded shipping/ fulfillment abilities across different carriers/ providers and local warehouses, exemplifying their ability to "unlocking every opportunity and filling every important gap in the system to give our merchants the best chance of success is one of our superpowers" while ensuring "resilience in the face of potential economic shifts."

These reasons may also be why SHOP has been reporting increased penetration in the non-core end markets (including health/ beauty, home/ garden, food/ beverage, animals/ pet supplies, and arts/ entertainment), as the management hints at "particularly strong growth rates in Q1."

These are significantly aided by their strategic decision to lengthen the paid free trial duration from one to three months in late 2024, with the increased cross selling and aggressive merchant acquisition approaches already triggering Shopify Payments' growing GMV penetration at 64% (+4 points YoY).

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These reasons may also be why the consensus have raised their forward estimates, with SHOP expected to chart an accelerated top/ bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +21.5%/ +25.7% through FY2027.

This is compared to the original estimates of +18.3%/ +14.2% (a year ago), albeit decelerating compared to the 5Y historical growth profile at a CAGR of +41.3%/ +111.2%, respectively.

For now, we are not overly concerned about its maturing growth profile, since SHOP's SaaS business model has triggered its increasingly rich cash flow/ Free Cash Flow investing thesis, as observed in the robust LTM numbers at $1.74B (+61.1% sequentially) and $1.72B (+63.8% sequentially) along with FY2024 numbers at $1.61B (+71.2% YoY) and $1.5B (+76.6% YoY), respectively.

These have already led to the company's healthier balance sheet with a growing net cash position of $4.59B (inline QoQ/ +7.7% YoY), underscoring how their "eighth straight quarter of delivering pro forma revenue growth of 25% or greater" have been directly bottom-line accretive.

SHOP Valuations

Thanks to the accelerated bottom-line growth projections, we can understand why the market has gotten exuberant surrounding SHOP's prospects, as observed in the premium FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 78.87x, not too far from its 1Y mean of 68.84x while moderated from its 5Y mean of 300.73x.

On the other hand, when compared to its peer group P/E mean of ~40x, it is apparent that the stock is trading at a great premium, with the recent recovery likely occurring overly fast and furious, as the bulls celebrated its robust FQ1'25 performance aided by the more than decent consumer spending trends, based on:

  • Amazon (AMZN) at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 34.20x,
  • MercadoLibre (MELI) at 47.85x,
  • Wix.com Ltd. (WIX) at 20.70x, and
  • BigCommerce (BIGC) at 17.89x.

The same has been observed in its relatively elevated FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 2.46x compared to its commerce peers as well, including AMZN at 2.04x, MELI at 1.63x, WIX at 1.19x, and BIGC at 1.20x.

This development implies that a moderate pullback to our Buy zones may be necessary for an improved return profile, one that we shall further discuss in the next segment.

So, Is SHOP Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

SHOP 5Y Stock Price

For now, SHOP has already charted an impressive recovery by +48.4% since the April 2025 bottom, as the stock also runs away from its 50/ 100/ 200 day moving averages to retest the next resistance levels of $114s ranges.

It is apparent that the rally has occurred rather rapidly, with the stock trading at a notable premium compared to our prior fair value estimates of $87.20.

Even so, based on the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $2.50 and the 1Y P/E mean of 68.84x (compared to its peer group P/E mean of ~40x), there appears to be an excellent upside potential of +50.7% to our bull-case long-term price target of $172.10.

SHOP's bullish thesis has been well supported by the three established uptrend support lines since October 2022 as well, with it offering interested investors with great insights into its future trading pattern.

Based on this trend, we are likely to see the stock chart new heights nearing the top trend line before eventually returning much of those gains to bounce at the lower trend line over the next few months or so.

SHOP Nearing The Overbought Levels

Our conjecture is not overly bearish as well, given SHOP's drastically higher short interest volumes by +38.2% on a YoY basis, worsened by the greedy stock market sentiments.

This is on top of the stock nearing overbought levels, as observed in the increasingly elevated Money Flow Index & Williams %R Momentum indexes above, with the historical up/ downtrend cycles further highlighting why investors looking to add should remain patient.

As a result, while we may reiterate our prior Buy rating for the SHOP stock, it comes with the caveat the investors only do so upon a moderate retracement to its prior $85 and $100s ranges for an improved margin of safety.

data.title: Anywhere Real Estate: Excessive Leverage Amid A Bear Market
data.description:
Anywhere Real Estate (NYSE:HOUS) is an American real estate franchising company that operates through brands such as Century21, Better Homes and Gardens Real Estate, Coldwell Banker, Sotheby's, and others. Its segments are divided into three: Advisors, Brands and Integrated Services. The Brands segment is the engine of its business, characterized by low operating costs and a network of independent brokers who operate with greater autonomy. However, the majority of its revenue (82%) comes from the Advisors segment, which is showing unfavorable margin trends and carries higher operating costs.

To fully analyze HOUS, as I did in my previous article, we must first understand the challenges facing the United States real estate sector, particularly the segment of models based on franchises rather than REITs. Market pessimism and elevated mortgage rates in the United States are helping to sustain depressed stock prices. Additionally, traditional brands are under pressure from innovative startups that aim to reduce intermediation, such as Compass, to which I will dedicate a separate article.

Even so, I believe some of these companies present interesting metrics and demonstrate more than acceptable operating efficiency in these lean times. That said, HOUS, in my opinion, is overleveraged relative to its cash flow and continues to report net losses. I don't see it in a favorable position for either buying or holding. For now, I recommend a sell, at least until we see an improvement in its debt profile.

Company Overview

Before moving forward with my analysis, I believe it's important to understand HOUS's internal business, in order to assess both its strengths and challenges. Century21 is the platform I'm most enthusiastic about, as it has the largest number of HOUS agents worldwide (219,329). However, it accounts for only half of HOUS's annual transactions. In contrast, Coldwell Banker, which forms the based of the Advisors segment, records 468,004 annual sales, marking it the largest generator of transactions in the United States.

The Advisors segment generates the highest revenue per transaction, with gross commission income of $19,720. However, this segment also incurs the highest costs within HOUS, which results in a rather underwhelming operating profit margin. In fact, it's a point of concern.

HOUS's profitability is largely driven by its franchise model, which delivers a strong EBITDA margin of 54%. However, 82% of its revenue comes from gross commission income within the Advisors segment (Owned Brokerage Group), composed of offices owned by the company that manage real estate transactions and carry substantial fixed costs.

According to the company's latest report for 1Q2025, $990 million of its $1.2 billion in total revenue came from this segment, an 8% increase year-over-year. However, the segment reported a negative operating EBITDA of $47 million.

Furthermore, I want to note that the only segment with positive operating EBITDA is Brands (Franchise Group), which posted $97 million in 1Q2025, an 8% year-over-year increase. I believe Century21's role as a franchise with virtually no operating costs plays a key part in this performance. However, this segment still lacks the scale to significantly impact the company's total revenue.

What stands out about HOUS is its solid global franchise network and its ability to sustain stable revenue despite a bear environment, elevated mortgage rates ranging between 6% and 7%, and growing competition from startups models that reduce intermediation. Even under these conditions, the company maintains an impressive EBITDA margin of 54% and secures new contracts with a typical term of 10 years.

Regarding its active agents, HOUS has a global total of 311,000, with 176,000 based in the United States. That number drops significantly when considering only offices directly operated by the company, which employ just 52,000 agents. In terms of trends, the United States agent count declined from 184,000 in 1Q2024 to 176,000 in 1Q2025, a smaller but consistent decline experienced by all traditional franchises.

Thesis

I want to emphasize that my sell thesis is not so much focused on HOUS's ability to weather short-term challenges in the real sector, but rather on its capital structure and the significant difficulties it faces in managing long-term debt given its current cash flow.

HOUS's total debt is $2.66 billion, compared to just $110 million in cash. Adding to this, the company reported a net loss of $78 million in 1Q2025 and a slightly negative EBITDA of $1 million. These figures are concerning at first glance. That said, the company is actively working to offset this imbalance by extending debt maturities further into the future.

Not only did they manage to extend debt maturities, but they also reduced total debt from $3.47 billion in 2019 to $2.94 billion in 2022. Additionally, they lowered the average fixed interest rate from 6.65% to 4.95%, effectively reducing the long-term cost of debt repayment.

Even so, their debt ratios, as shown in Seeking Alpha's financials section, highlight ongoing concerns. The Total Debt/Equity ratio stands at 211%, far above the sector average of 96%. The same applies to the Total Debt/Capital ratio, which is 67% versus the sector average of 49%.

When breaking down the company's debt structure, it should be noted that payments are well distributed through 2030. The nearest significant maturity is in June 2026, totaling $403 million. Given HOUS's current cash position, it would likely be unable to meet this payment without refinancing.

One potential source of liquidity is the Revolving Credit Facility, which has a total capacity of $1.1 billion, but as of March 31, 2025, $610 million had already been used, leaving approximately $460 million available to cover the 2026 maturity. However, this facility itself matures in July 2027, representing another short-term financial hurdle.

A further concern, in my opinion, is the Net Debt Leverage Ratio of 7.2x, a level of indebtedness that is particularly difficult to manage in a bearish market like the current real estate environment, especially given HOUS's weak cash generation.

1Q2025

Although I've already highlighted some figures from HOUS's latest report, I'd like to go into more detail about its cost and revenue structure. Previously, I broke down performance by segment, now, I want to focus specifically on revenue composition. Gross commission income reached $976 million in 1Q2025, up from $907 million in the same quarter of the previous year.

Commissions remain HOUS's primary revenue source, which is a positive indicator. In fact, other revenue streams also posted year-over-year growth. However, the challenge lies in the operating cost structure, particularly the ongoing difficulty of sustaining profitability within the Advisors segment.

In this context, I'd like to assess the company's cost reduction strategy, which anticipates savings of $100 million by 2025. Management reports that a total of $660 million in cost reductions were achieved between 2020 and 2024. However, the $100 million goal seems modest, especially considering that annual savings in 2024 were higher. If achieved, this new target would still leave total operating expenses around $1.6 billion.

Valuation

Now I want to show you a discounted cash flow (DCF) model I built using data provided by HOUS, figures from Seeking Alpha's financials section, and my own estimations. I applied a 27% tax rate, consistent with the latest figures reported on SA. For the terminal growth rate, I used 2%, which is conservative and below the historical average of global GDP growth. As for the WACC, I used a 10% rate, reflecting the company's recent efforts to lower its average fixed interest rate.

For D&A, I'm using a moderate annual rate of 5%, considering the company's limited capacity for the new investments. I started with $184 million for 2025, which I derived by annualizing the $46 million reported in D&A for 1Q2025. For CAPEX, I'm also annualizing the starting figure of $80 million. I'm applying a -4% annual decline rate, based on the assumption that the company will continue its efforts to reduce operating costs.

Similarly, assuming improved operational efficiency, I applied a 4% annual growth rate to EBIT, aiming to take an optimistic view of the company's performance. I used $92 million as the starting EBIT for my model, based on the 2024 10-Q report. That report shows operating EBITDA of $290 million, from which I subtracted $198 million in D&A to arrive at my starting figure.

Based on these assumptions, the DCF model suggests that HOUS could be worth nearly 73% more than its current share price. However, this apparent upside is entirely offset by the company's substantial debt load.

Risks

I've already explained that total debt and its leverage are the main reasons for my caution regarding HOUS. This remains the core risk for the company, as it does not generate sufficient net income or cash flow to support a clear path to financial improvement.

Added to this, the risk inherent in the pessimistic situation in the United States real estate sector, high mortgage rates, and technological peers that are reducing intermediation. It's also worth noting that even among legacy players, HOUS stands out for its elevated debt burden; several of its peers maintain healthier balance sheets, which places HOUS at a relative disadvantage.

Finally, it's important to consider the impact of FED interest rates. If rates remain elevated, or rise further, HOUS's leverage will become more problematic, as a portion of its debt is tied to variable rates.

Conclusion

While HOUS's outlook is not encouraging, it largely mirrors the broader challenges facing the United States real estate sector, particularly in a model based on franchises. HOUS has a robust network of leading global franchises, and in a more favorable environment, with more intensive cost reduction and lower debt leverage, I have no doubt its rating could improve. However, I don't see that scenario materializing in the near term. In conclusion, I maintain a sell rating on the stock
